title: LIVE - State of the Union Postgame Analysis (with @ponderingpolitics)
summary: Did you miss Trump's State of the Union speech? Or — did you see it, and need a place to vent? Join us LIVE, as Jessica Tarlov and Jessiah Eberlin of @ponderingpolitics  discuss, analyze, and pick apart the speech's notable moments.
transcript_html:
YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NTQT9-gINZE

Transcript

longest ever in American history. I I felt it. Did you feel it? >> Every sec. I had two monsters today and a nap to prepare. And I'm not even 35 yet. And it's just like spiritually >> and I now I'm on a diet Pepsi. Like I am overcaffeinated in the extreme. But that's what it took to get through >> whatever that was. It was awful. He said 250 years and it felt like that was the length of the speech and not the country, you know? 40 minutes or something? 45 minutes. Like it was just bad campaign stop stuff. Like, you know, when he has those full schedule days

where they're like, "Donald Trump went from like in the morning until at night, but you're at his rally at the end of the day, and he's just totally zonked, but he's going to make you endure it because he's that much of an egoomaniac." I have nothing but

confidence in this. Um it's um the one thing I think you're going to look back on this, Scott's going to be so jealous. Scott's going to be like, "Wait, I chose to sleep. stuff like that. He is a walking cure for insomnia at this point, which is weird because it's the exact opposite of how he used to be. Also, welcome to Raging Moderates, our first live stream. I'm Jessica Tarlov with Josiah from Pondering Politics. Um, excited to be here. Try this out. All the cool kids are doing it. work right now and maybe you find this later and you know that we did it. Um, but to the energy point, I don't know if you noticed the first like 10 or 12 minutes where it really felt like he was never going to get a hit of dopamine, but he did perk up about the hockey team >> um, when they came in. Um, so what did you make of kind the entire saga, I guess, and the moment that they had there, which was pretty, you know, bipartisan round of applause. Obviously, welldeserved, very exciting, um, to win the gold in men's and women's hockey. Um, what did you think? And listen, you know, he he definitely revolutionized the state of

the union as we know it in terms of like some of the stunts that he'll pull. He still has an instinct for certain things. I thought just as a student of politics, it was brilliant to bring in the the hockey team there because to your point, he knows the Democrats are understandably understandably not going to applaud or rise for him. And I just got to say to the people watching, when I hear conservatives complain about this, it's like, y'all didn't stand for Biden. What's good for the goose is good for the gander. So if you want to complain about that, your ass better be standing the next time a Democrat is in

there from start to finish. Otherwise, shut the [ __ ] up. But it was still very clever of him to do that because he knew it would be a terrible look for the Democrats to stay seated and predictably they stood up and applauded and that was a rare moment of bipartisan consensus which of course Trump doesn't typically value that sort of thing but you know he wants it during the State of the Union as a roundabout way to own the lib. So using the the hockey team I think cynically as a prop was actually brilliant politics. totally there with you and I I do feel bad for them in a

certain way because I I think that when they got on the phone with him uh celebrating after the win with Cash Patel in there, they did not think that they were being going to be launched into a political conversation like this. >> And of the five players who didn't come from the men's teams, they have a good excuse because their NHL season starts again tomorrow. I thought it was very interesting. Four of the five are from Minnesota. Oh, I did not know that. Good. >> I saw that floating around. And you know, I'm sure people want their beauty rest. a state that has been rocked by such political controversy and the tragedy that we've seen unfold there um with the overreach and deadly misuse of force on behalf of ICE that uh that weighed heavily on them in making their decision about this. disappointing because I was waiting for that. >> Sad. He's just stuck in the kids section looking for a tiny jacket. Mhm. >> Um, what did you think overall? I mean, this is hard because it was it was so long, but I guess my top line, if I were to throw it out there, um, bad on the economy, good on the culture stuff, which is kind of to be expected. Um, his political instincts definitely kicking into high gear with the question about, you know, like stand up if you want to protect American lives over the lives of illegals and got it so that Democrats were sitting down for

that. Um, I already I'm going doing the five tomorrow. >> It's gonna be my life. I I know. >> God speed. >> C. Can I just say, you know, far be for me to give you notes, but I I do hope I'm sorry. essence of Donald Trump 2.0 because I think Donald Trump 1.0 in the first term actually held out some hope that he'd get support from Democrats on a couple of issues and he did. of people virtue signaling about what they were offended by. No one's offended by anything anymore. or they're going to get yelled at by somebody or that Trump's going to single them out. So they don't they don't do it. They don't do what they know is right. saw a little I shouldn't say I was going to say a lot. So, a little is actually correct. Like unease on people's faces where they're like, "Well, what do we do now?" Right? Like I don't know. independence, particularly during the guest standing up. Then you have that to consider as well. One thing I noted is, did you see Tom Swisy? There was a couple of times that he showed up on camera. snoozing. >> Yeah. No, that's what I'm saying. So, it was it was really tough. As I I have to remind people all the time, Donald Trump is not a Gen Z

influencer. He's not just some random guy. He's not even a successful pundit. He is the most powerful man on earth. And I subscribe to the Uncle Ben from Spider-Man school of power and responsibility. With great power comes great responsibility. The greatest power, the greatest responsibility. It's bad enough coming from him, but his entire party, Jess, they all stood and applauded when he was talking about Democrats can only win if they if they cheat in elections. And the midterms are coming up. So what does that do but send the most dystopian signal to resistance libs everywhere? Well, like [ __ ] we're

on track to win the midterms, all else being equal. So what are you suggesting that you and your party will do if we win? Are you just not going to seed us? Are you going to deny the results? It's bad enough when it comes from Trump, but when the rest of the party stands up on live TV, yes, we're we're perfect and we we're the only legitimate winners of any election ever. And the only way they can win is they is if they cheat. That's really really dangerous. And I think that was the worst part of the entire State of the Union. >> I agree with you. They didn't like his third term joke, I will note. Um so we're kind of off that cliff a little

bit. Um but cheating your way to invalidating the results of the election is a very very real threat. And I've been thinking about it a lot. obviously it'll be 2027, so it'll be two years before that, but it is a big deal. And to your point, the two people, you know, the two people sitting behind Trump, right? election thieves standing behind him. telling big corporations to figure it out themselves, there must be some sort of kickback involved in here and it can't be above board. Number one, he always cracks me up when he's like >> it's like 500% 900%. >> It's like that's not how math works. And

I'm not a math guy, right? If you saw my ACTs, they were heavily lopsided towards the language arts as opposed to >> And you took ACTs. >> Yeah, the I did. Well, I took the SATs and the ACTs, but the ACTs were the ones that uh were the ones that got me into college. But it's like I'm not a math guy, but even I know the most you can do is 100% and anything past that, it's free. And now they're actively paying you to take the damn drug. So he he just doubled down on that. And I'm just like, I I don't know what you're thinking about peddling those numbers. The MAGA base will eat it up. will either. But the other reason that I have an issue with Trump RX is, man, it's I don't want I don't want to call it a scam. and then it will often redirect you to the thing itself. Right? So, it's just it's not what President Biden was doing in the inflation reduction act, which was actually forcing pharmaceutical companies and Medicare to negotiate prescription drug prices. the cost of well so like number one I don't want to hear about communism and Marxism from the Republican party ever again >> even after they pay him >> even element of the uh state capitalism but yes >> correct number two how much are they going to absorb 100% of the disparity are they going to only partially absorb um some of the the the energy cost disparity and which companies because he's suspiciously silent about that he's just like these companies and so it's kind of like his whole $18 trillion investment thing. It's such [ __ ] It's been debunked to Helen back. Even

the White House website only claims about 9.6 trillion. So they're they're about 10 trillion off. And even that it's it's counting trade deals and Biden era investment commitments. He's just full of [ __ ] And he's hoping that the American people won't notice. And I used to believe that that was his superpower. However, the Biden era taught me this because I remember when when people would talk about Biden's economy, and I was one of them. I was guilty of this. I'm thinking GDP is great under Biden. these macroeconomic metrics. That's not how we look at the at it these days. The American people maybe gave a [ __ ] about that once, but they do not anymore. It's about the cost of things. And so, I think he's lost his ability to [ __ ] the American people with these lies. I think they're going to be like, "Milk is costing more than it was, and you said you would cut costs on day one. a different contrast, but it was interesting to me to see the difference between how he was this afternoon. hasn't been a magical fixing of everything. And then he comes out tonight completely hot. Right. Yeah. >> Uh and I like that. I should do that. I should do that on the five tomorrow. Like I'm done. Um but I thought that that was the real missed opportunity of the evening. Even asking for 20 seconds of humility for understanding the experience of average Americans is too much for him because his ego is so big. And that's a blessing for us. incredible polling work. Uh we're doing live uh dials and focus groups as this was going on. Nothing tanked approval more than when he railed on about tariffs. Yeah. >> Right. >> Yeah. Oh, I >> I believe that's the case. >> Okay. So I that was interesting to me like the work that he had to do which is purely based in the economy he did not do and the state of the union

increasingly doesn't matter for much but that was the only thing that I was really afraid of that he could potentially move the ball on um because it would show also an ability to speak accurately about people's lived experiences which the Biden administration and a lot of us who were messaging about it not on behalf of the administration. Because when I

was when I gave my announcement, I'm like when I covered the Supreme Court decision, he had not yet publicly reacted. And I told my audience, I said, "Listen, there was always there is always a possibility, however unlikely, that Trump will be persuaded to take this legal defeat and extract a political victory because you could easily make the case that I I'm a very I'm a cynic by nature when it comes to the Supreme Court. Again, it's a bug bear of mind. I could easily see in the back of their minds that that Roberts, Amy Coney Barrett, and Gorsuch were in part trying to do Trump a favor. you're

going to get your ass kicked in the midterms if these tariffs are still in place. >> But to your point, >> he is so thin skinned. He would ra he just cut his nose off despite his face. The smart the wiser prudent thing to do would be like, "I love my tariffs. I wanted the tariffs. They're amazing. They did all this and just do the usual lies. But in this country, we have to defer to the Supreme Court or whatever he wanted to say. And so reluctantly, I'm withdrawing the ter whatever." But he won't do it. Instead, he's like, "They're going to be 10% tariffs, 15% tariffs, and if you cross me, it's going to be even more than that." And it's

just like, I mean, it's going to be damaging to the economy. So, as a consumer and as somebody who loves consumers, family members, and friends, and everybody else, that sucks. But as a partisan Democrat, it's like, >> well done, sir. That is that is precisely what you should do, please. Immigration is always kind of a a sore

point. Um, especially when you're talking about families that have lost loved ones um to, you know, violent illegals uh who were in the country. Um, but I've been I don't want to say just lately because I have been upset for a long time that it has not felt like our party has been putting up the goods, right? Not only in leadership style, but actually in policy to counteract what it is that the Republicans are offering. There's a lot of just keeping the status quo or undoing what Trump did. Um, so where do you think is this is the official

kickoff right of campaign season? Um, what do you think the Democrats have to do or can do to counteract what the Republicans are offering or the fact that the Republicans exist or that people could stay home? I guess, >> you know, my analysis, I'm I'm always open to data which contradicts me. So, I just want to preface this. You know, I I try to have some degree of epistemic humility about this, but here's how I look at it. Donald Trump is president at a bare minimum, barring some sort of like health issue until January 20th, 2029.

And that means best case scenario, let's say we flip the House and the Senate is increasingly in play and we're getting reporting that they're terrified about that. So let's say we take both because you don't have the three political chambers, the the Congress, both chambers of Congress and the presidency. Democrats can't enact an affirmative agenda. And I think they have to be transparent about that. like the affirmative agenda I think comes in the 2028 campaign cycle. I think for 2026 they need to keep doing what they're doing which is and this is unfortunately for us or rather fortunately for us I

should say it's an easier job. It's a referendum on Trump. It's a referendum on the Republican party. I think the American people recognize on some level well [ __ ] if Democrats take control of the House. What are they going to do? Anything they want to do is going to get killed in the Senate if they don't take the Senate or Trump will veto it. So it's it's dead on arrival. So, they have to campaign on checking Trump's affordability crisis. Hey, listen. Those tariffs, for example, that the Supreme Court struck down and now he's doubling down on because he's going to find alternative ways to do it. We're going to do everything in our power if you give us the House and ideally the Senate to prevent him or overturn those tariffs

legislatively. We'll have votes. We'll have um hearings one after another on his corruption. And I think that they should be running much harder on a corruption angle as well while keeping affordability the top issue because the only two issues that Democrats can do anything about in the age of Trump is trying to check his affordability crisis and investigating his corruption. Affirmatively there's no legislative agenda that they can offer that Trump will accept. So that's what I think the goal needs to be. They need to do what Mikey Cheryl did, Abigail Spamberger did, Zoran Mani. You're talking about various points of the political
